refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting and verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle sensor acquisition → telemetry reception at operator console) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using instrumented test harnesses. Collect p95 statistics over minimum 1000-sample windows per test scenario.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz sampling rate.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit encoded telemetry to the operator console with network transmission latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive operator commands at the vehicle with network reception latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and respond to safety-critical faults (loss of signal, latency threshold breach) within 100 ms.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dedicated low-latency network stack with priority queuing, adaptive codec selection, and edge-local processing to minimize serialization and transmission delays.
DP-B: Deploy hybrid architecture combining local autonomous vehicle control with remote operator override, reducing dependency on round-trip latency for safety-critical maneuvers.
DP-C: Establish redundant communication paths (primary + failover) with automatic switchover to maintain latency SLA under network degradation.
DP-D: Implement predictive state extrapolation at operator console to mask network latency through client-side interpolation of vehicle state.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A + DP-B (integrated): Implement a dedicated low-latency network stack with priority queuing and adaptive codec selection (DP-A) combined with hybrid local autonomy + remote override architecture (DP-B) to achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms while maintaining safety margins through local vehicle intelligence.

potential_sources: DP-A (network stack) couples to vehicle communication hardware and operator console UI responsiveness.
DP-B (hybrid autonomy) couples to vehicle motion control, safety services (fault detection), and operator command interpretation.
Integration point: Vehicle must expose real-time state API compatible with low-latency codec; operator console must support both autonomous and override modes.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is manageable via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). DP-A and DP-B are complementary: network optimization reduces latency floor; hybrid autonomy reduces latency sensitivity for non-critical operations.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions remain within agreed envelope (bandwidth ≥ X Mbps, jitter ≤ Y ms, packet loss ≤ Z%).
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle sensors (cameras, LiDAR, IMU) can be sampled and encoded at ≥ 10 Hz without exceeding network bandwidth.
RISK: Adaptive codec selection may introduce variable latency if codec switching logic is not deterministic; mitigation: pre-qualify codec profiles offline.
RISK: Hybrid autonomy introduces complexity in mode transitions (autonomous → override); mitigation: define explicit handoff protocols and test extensively.
RISK: Operator console UI responsiveness may not match network latency improvements if rendering pipeline is slow; mitigation: profile and optimize UI event loop.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify codec selection strategy (e.g., H.264 for video, delta-encoding for sensor telemetry) and priority queue discipline (e.g., safety alerts > telemetry > non-critical data). Refine DP-B to define autonomous capability scope (e.g., obstacle avoidance, speed limiting) vs. operator-controlled scope (e.g., precise positioning, tool operation).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates end-to-end teleoperation logic: vehicle-side telemetry acquisition and encoding, network transmission/reception, operator-side command decoding and UI rendering, and latency monitoring/fault response. Owns the low-latency network stack, codec selection, and hybrid autonomy mode management.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module emits encoded vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data, latency metrics) to operator console and safety services. Format: low-latency codec (H.264 video + delta-encoded sensor telemetry). Frequency: ≥ 10 Hz.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from operator console. Format: command messages with timestamp and priority. Latency requirement: ≤ 75 ms (p95) from operator input to vehicle reception.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives safety constraints from Safety Services (e.g., geofence, speed limits, no-go zones) and emits latency breach alerts, signal loss events, and mode transition notifications. Latency: ≤ 100 ms for fault detection and response.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with physical network (WiFi, cellular, satellite) via Network Link abstraction. Owns priority queuing and adaptive codec logic above this interface. Network Link provides bandwidth, jitter, and packet loss metrics.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of latency-critical logic. All telemetry encoding, command decoding, and latency monitoring are cohesive within this module. Minimal diffusion: only Network Link abstraction and Safety Services integration points.

Vehicle module owns motion control and sensor hardware but should not own telemetry encoding or network stack logic. Assigning DP-A (network stack) to Vehicle would create tight coupling to communication protocols and diffuse latency responsibility across vehicle and operator domains.

Safety Services owns fault detection and constraint enforcement but should not own telemetry transmission or command reception. Latency monitoring and network optimization are outside Safety Services scope.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but too coarse-grained for detailed latency optimization. Assigning to NGHE Solution would defer architectural decisions and diffuse responsibility.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it encapsulates the full teleoperation data flow (vehicle → network → operator), (2) it owns the low-latency network stack and codec selection logic, (3) it integrates with Safety Services for fault response, and (4) it minimizes diffusion by centralizing latency-critical concerns.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it owns both vehicle-side and operator-side logic; mitigation: decompose into Vehicle Telemetry Agent (vehicle-side) and Operator Console (operator-side) at next decomposition level, with Teleoperation as the coordinating module.

RISK: Network Link abstraction may not provide sufficient visibility into network conditions for adaptive codec selection; mitigation: ensure Network Link exports bandwidth, jitter, and packet loss metrics in real-time.

RISK: Hybrid autonomy logic (DP-B) may require tight coupling to Vehicle motion control; mitigation: define clear handoff protocol between autonomous and override modes, owned by Teleoperation but executed by Vehicle.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has explicit ownership of latency budgeting and monitoring. Define sub-modules (Vehicle Telemetry Agent, Operator Console, Network Coordinator) at next level to distribute implementation while maintaining cohesion. Ensure Network Link interface exports real-time network metrics (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) for adaptive codec logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP (low-latency network stack + hybrid autonomy) directly addresses FR (p95 latency ≤ 150 ms). DP-A targets network transmission/reception latency (≤ 75 ms each); DP-B reduces latency sensitivity through local autonomy. Combined approach is sound and verifiable. No independence violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module owns both vehicle-side (telemetry acquisition) and operator-side (command reception) logic; risk of diffusion if not cleanly separated at next level.
Hybrid autonomy (DP-B) requires coordination with Vehicle motion control; risk of unclear ownership boundaries if Vehicle module also owns autonomous behavior.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the correct owner (containment_score 95). Diffusion risks are manageable through explicit sub-module decomposition at next level (Vehicle Telemetry Agent, Operator Console, Network Coordinator). Network Link interface must provide real-time metrics for adaptive codec logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: latency can be measured end-to-end using instrumented test harnesses. p95 metric is well-defined and testable. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) provide clear verification targets.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns latency optimization and monitoring. Safety Services owns fault detection and constraint enforcement. Vehicle owns motion control and sensor hardware. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-A to specify codec profiles (H.264 for video, delta-encoding for telemetry) and priority queue discipline (safety alerts > telemetry > non-critical data).
Refine DP-B to define autonomous capability scope (obstacle avoidance, speed limiting) vs. operator-controlled scope (precise positioning, tool operation).

At next decomposition level, explicitly decompose Teleoperation into Vehicle Telemetry Agent (vehicle-side), Operator Console (operator-side), and Network Coordinator (network optimization) to reduce diffusion risk.
Ensure Network Link interface exports real-time network metrics (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) for adaptive codec selection logic.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) address distinct concerns: telemetry acquisition, network transmission, command reception, and fault response. Each sub-FR requires detailed DP selection and IM assignment. Teleoperation module is the correct owner, but internal architecture (Vehicle Telemetry Agent, Operator Console, Network Coordinator) must be refined at next level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz sampling rate.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit encoded telemetry to the operator console with network transmission latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive operator commands at the vehicle with network reception latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and respond to safety-critical faults (loss of signal, latency threshold breach) within 100 ms.

assumptions: Network conditions remain within agreed envelope (bandwidth ≥ X Mbps, jitter ≤ Y ms, packet loss ≤ Z%).
Vehicle sensors can be sampled and encoded at ≥ 10 Hz without exceeding network bandwidth.
Operator console UI event loop can respond to commands within 50 ms (leaving 25 ms margin for network latency).
Hybrid autonomy mode transitions can be executed deterministically without introducing additional latency.
Safety Services can enforce constraints (geofence, speed limits) without blocking telemetry transmission.
open_risks: Adaptive codec selection may introduce variable latency if codec switching logic is not deterministic.
Hybrid autonomy introduces complexity in mode transitions (autonomous → override); unclear handoff protocol.
Operator console UI responsiveness may not match network latency improvements if rendering pipeline is slow.
Network Link abstraction may not provide sufficient visibility into network conditions for real-time codec adaptation.
Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if vehicle-side and operator-side logic are not cleanly separated.
evidence_needed: Network latency measurements under agreed conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Codec profile benchmarks (latency, bandwidth, quality trade-offs for H.264, VP9, delta-encoding).
Operator console UI event loop profiling (input latency, rendering latency, total response time).
Hybrid autonomy mode transition test results (determinism, safety margins, operator override responsiveness).
Network Link interface specification (real-time metrics export, priority queue discipline, adaptive codec API).
Sub-module decomposition design (Vehicle Telemetry Agent, Operator Console, Network Coordinator interfaces and responsibilities).
